title: Kinetic molecular theory
description: Check your understanding of the kinetic molecular theory in this set of free practice questions designed for AP Chemistry students.

assessment_items.question_content:
The diagram below shows the distribution of speeds for a sample of $\ce{Ne}(g)$ atoms at $\pu{273 K}$ and $\pu{1 atm}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following best shows how the distribution curve changes when the sample is heated to a higher temperature?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints: The Kelvin temperature of a gas sample is directly proportional to the average kinetic energy of the particles in the sample. This means that when the sample of $\ce{Ne}(g)$ atoms is heated to a higher temperature, the average kinetic energy of the atoms increases.
Since kinetic energy is directly proportional to the square of velocity ($KE = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$), the average speed of the atoms also increases.

As a result of this higher average speed, the peak of the distribution curve shifts to the right, towards a higher value. Additionally, because the area under the curve remains constant, the entire curve becomes shorter and broader.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

So, the correct answer is


[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt: A graph with number of particles on the y-axis and speed on the x-axis. A solid line begins at the origin and curves up until it reaches a maximum half-way up the y-axis and 1/3 of the way along the x-axis. The line then gradually curves down until it reaches the x-axis near the end of the x-axis.

The line then gradually curves down until it reaches the x-axis near the end of the x-axis. assessment_items.question_content:
The diagram below shows the distribution of speeds for a sample of $\ce{CH4}(g)$ molecules at $\pu{273 K}$ and $\pu{1 atm}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following best shows how the distribution curve changes when the sample is cooled to a lower temperature?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints: The Kelvin temperature of a gas sample is directly proportional to the average kinetic energy of the particles in the sample. This means that when the sample of $\ce{CH4}(g)$ molecules is cooled to a lower temperature, the average kinetic energy of the molecules decreases.
Since kinetic energy is directly proportional to the square of velocity ($KE = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$), the average speed of the molecules also decreases.

As a result of this lower average speed, the peak of the distribution curve shifts to the left, toward a lower value. Additionally, because the area under the curve remains constant, the entire curve becomes taller and narrower.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

So, the correct answer is


[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt: A graph with number of particles on the y-axis and speed on the x-axis. A solid line begins at the origin and curves up until it reaches a maximum half-way up the y-axis and 1/3 of the way along the x-axis. The line then gradually curves down until it reaches the x-axis near the end of the x-axis.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following arranges the gases $\ce{CH4}$, $\ce{NF3}$, and $\ce{O2}$ in order of increasing average molecular speed at $\pu{298 K}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints: The average molecular speed of a gas is higher when the gas has a *higher* temperature and a *lower* molar mass.

Of the three gases, $\ce{NF3}$ has the highest molar mass, which means it has the *lowest* average molecular speed at $\pu{298 K}$. In contrast, $\ce{CH4}$ has the lowest molar mass, which means it has the *highest* average molecular speed at $\pu{298 K}$.
So, the correct arrangement is $\ce{NF3} < \ce{O2} < \ce{CH4}$.

choices.content: $\ce{NF3} < \ce{O2} < \ce{CH4}$
$\ce{CH4} < \ce{O2} < \ce{NF3}$
$\ce{O2} < \ce{CH4} < \ce{NF3}$
$\ce{NF3} < \ce{CH4} < \ce{O2}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following arranges the gases $\ce{H2S}$, $\ce{CO}$, and $\ce{Cl2}$ in order of increasing average molecular speed at $\pu{298 K}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints: The average molecular speed of a gas is higher when the gas has a *higher* temperature and a *lower* molar mass.

Of the three gases, $\ce{Cl2}$ has the highest molar mass, which means it has the *lowest* average molecular speed at $\pu{298 K}$. In contrast, $\ce{CO}$ has the lowest molar mass, which means it has the *highest* average molecular speed at $\pu{298 K}$.
So, the correct arrangement is $\ce{Cl2} < \ce{H2S} < \ce{CO}$.

choices.content: $\ce{Cl2} < \ce{H2S} < \ce{CO}$
$\ce{CO} < \ce{H2S} < \ce{Cl2}$
$\ce{H2S} < \ce{Cl2} < \ce{CO}$
$\ce{CO} < \ce{Cl2} < \ce{H2S}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following arranges the gases $\ce{C2H4}$, $\ce{F2}$, and $\ce{SO2}$ in order of increasing average molecular speed at $\pu{298 K}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints: The average molecular speed of a gas is higher when the gas has a *higher* temperature and a *lower* molar mass.

Of the three gases, $\ce{SO2}$ has the highest molar mass, which means it has the *lowest* average molecular speed at $\pu{298 K}$. In contrast, $\ce{C2H4}$ has the lowest molar mass, which means it has the *highest* average molecular speed at $\pu{298 K}$.
So, the correct arrangement is $\ce{SO2} < \ce{F2} < \ce{C2H4}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The diagram below represents three samples of $\ce{He}(g)$ in identical rigid containers. The lengths of the arrows indicate the speeds of the atoms in each sample.


[[☃ image 1]]

**Based on the diagram, the pressure of the $\ce{He}(g)$ is**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
For a particular gas, pressure varies based on the number of gas particles in a given volume and the average speed of the particles. The more particles of gas and the higher their average speed, the higher the pressure.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
Let’s start by comparing the number of $\ce{He}(g)$ atoms in each container. According to the diagram, there are *six* atoms in containers 2 and 3 and only *four* in container 1.

Next, let’s compare the average speed of the $\ce{He}(g)$ atoms in each container. In the diagram, the relative speed of each atom is indicated by an arrow, with longer arrows corresponding to higher speeds. Looking at the arrows in each container collectively, we can see that the average speed of the atoms is *higher* in container 2 than in container 1 or 3.
Together, this means that the pressure of the $\ce{He}(g)$ is highest in container 2.

options.alt: Three boxes contain dots with arrows pointing in random directions. Each dot has an arrow with varying lengths. Box 1 contains 4 dots; 2 with medium length arrows and 2 with relatively short arrows. Box 2 contains 6 dots; 3 with relatively long arrows, 2 with medium length arrows and 1 with a short arrow. Box 3 contains 6 dots with 3 medium length and 3 relatively short arrows.

choices.content: highest in container 1
highest in container 2
the same in containers 2 and 3
the same in containers 1, 2, and 3

assessment_items.question_content:
The diagram below represents three samples of $\ce{Ne}(g)$ in identical rigid containers. The lengths of the arrows indicate the speeds of the atoms in each sample.


[[☃ image 1]]

**Based on the diagram, the pressure of the $\ce{Ne}(g)$ is**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
For a particular gas, pressure varies based on the number of gas particles in a given volume and the average speed of the particles. The fewer particles of gas and the lower their average speed, the lower the pressure.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
Let’s start by comparing the number of $\ce{Ne}(g)$ atoms in each container. According to the diagram, there are *four* atoms in containers 1 and 3 and a total of *six* in container 2.

Next, let’s compare the average speed of the $\ce{Ne}(g)$ atoms in each container. In the diagram, the relative speed of each atom is indicated by an arrow, with shorter arrows corresponding to lower speeds. Looking at the arrows in each container collectively, we can see that the average speed of the atoms is *lower* in container 1 than in container 2 or 3.
Together, this means that the pressure of the $\ce{Ne}(g)$ is lowest in container 1.

options.alt: Three boxes contain dots with arrows pointing in random directions. Each dot has an arrow with varying lengths. Box 1 contains 4 dots; 2 dots with medium length arrows and 2 dots with relatively short arrows. Box 2 contains 6 dots; 3 relatively long arrows, 2 medium length arrows and 1 with a relatively short arrow. Box 3 contains 4 dots; 2 with relatively long arrows, 1 with a medium arrow and 1 with a relatively short arrow.

choices.content: lowest in container 1
lowest in container 2
the same in containers 1 and 3
the same in containers 1, 2, and 3

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
For a particular gas, pressure varies based on the number of gas particles in a given volume and the average speed of the particles. The more particles of gas and the higher their average speed, the higher the pressure.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
Let’s start by comparing the number of $\ce{Ar}(g)$ atoms in each container. According to the diagram, there are *six* atoms in containers 1 and 3 and only *four* in container 2.

Next, let’s compare the average speed of the $\ce{Ar}(g)$ atoms in each container. In the diagram, the relative speed of each atom is indicated by an arrow, with longer arrows corresponding to higher speeds. Looking at the arrows in each container collectively, we can see that the average speed of the atoms is *higher* in container 3 than in container 1 or 2.
Together, this means that the pressure of the $\ce{Ar}(g)$ is highest in container 3.

options.alt: Three boxes contain arrows pointing in random directions. Each dot has an arrow with varying lengths. Box 1 contains 6 dots; 3 with medium length arrows and 3 with relatively short arrows. Box 2 contains 4 dots; 2 with medium length arrows and 2 with relatively short arrows. Box 3 contains 6 dots; 3 with relatively long arrows, 2 with medium length arrows and 1 with a relatively short arrow.

choices.content: highest in container 2
highest in container 3
the same in containers 1 and 3
the same in containers 1, 2, and 3

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider two identical vessels, one containing $\pu{1.5 mol}$ of $\ce{N2}(g)$ at $\pu{250 K}$ and the other containing $\pu{1.5 mol}$ of $\ce{N2}(g)$ at $\pu{350 K}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following statements is true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: Two boxes each contain 1.5 moles of N 2. Box 1 is at 250 K while box 2 is at 350 K.

choices.content: The rate at which $\ce{N2}(g)$ molecules collide with the vessel walls is lower in vessel 1 than in vessel 2.
The average speed of the $\ce{N2}(g)$ molecules is higher in vessel 1 than in vessel 2.
The pressure exerted by the $\ce{N2}(g)$ is the same in both vessels.
The average kinetic energy of the $\ce{N2}(g)$ molecules is the same in both vessels.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider two identical flasks, one containing $\pu{2.0 mol}$ of $\ce{O2}(g)$ at $\pu{175 K}$ and the other containing $\pu{2.0 mol}$ of $\ce{O2}(g)$ at $\pu{425 K}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following statements is true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: Two boxes each contain 2.0 moles of O 2. Box 1 is at 175 K while box 2 is at 425 K.

choices.content: The average speed of the $\ce{O2}(g)$ molecules is lower in flask 1 than in flask 2.
The rate at which $\ce{O2}(g)$ molecules collide with the flask walls is higher in flask 1 than in flask 2.
The pressure exerted by the $\ce{O2}(g)$ is the same in both flasks.
The average kinetic energy of the $\ce{O2}(g)$ molecules is the same in both flasks.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider two identical containers at $\pu{273 K}$, one filled with $\pu{3.0 mol}$ of $\ce{H2}(g)$ and the other filled with $\pu{1.0 mol}$ of $\ce{H2}(g)$.


[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following statements is true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: Two boxes each contain H 2 at 273 K. Box 1 contains 3.0 moles of H 2 while box 2 contains 1.0 mole of H 2.

choices.content: The rate at which $\ce{H2}(g)$ molecules collide with the container walls is higher in container 1 than in container 2.
The average speed of the $\ce{H2}(g)$ molecules is lower in container 1 than in container 2.
The pressure exerted by the $\ce{H2}(g)$ is the same in both containers.
The average kinetic energy of the $\ce{H2}(g)$ molecules is higher in container 1 than in container 2.
